<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] {{Nihongo|'''Kasuga Gongen Genki E'''|春日権現験記絵}} is a set of painted handscrolls ([[:en:Emakimono|Emakimono]]) that was produced during the early 14th Century ([[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]) of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], by members of the Fujiwara clan. The work was created in order to honor the deities of Kasuga, with the stories revolving around the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga shrine]] and [[Kōfuku-ji|Kōfukuji temple]], located in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. The colourful silk work was made with silver and gold paints and consists of 20 scrolls with 93 sections of text and illustrations, which was completed in 1309.
[[File:Kasuga gongen genki-e, Takashina Takakane, 1-3 (part 1).JPG|thumb|Kasuga Gongen Genki E]]
==Meaning of the name== The term “Gongen” means [[:en:Avatar|Avatar]] or [[Kami|Deity]], while the term “genki” in the title is actually short for “reigenki”; reigenki can be translated into “chronicles of marvelous and supernatural events”. Taken together, the title has been translated into “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity” or “Illustrated Tales of the Miracles of the Avatars of Kasuga” or the equivalent. And as the title suggests, the work focuses on miraculous events involving the Kasuga Shrine and Buddhist/Shinto deities.
==Authorship and history== The former regent ([[Sesshō and Kampaku|kanpaku]]), [[:en:Takatsukasa Mototada|Takatsukasa (Fujiwara) Mototada]], and his three sons wrote the original text, based on stories compiled by a monk named Kakuen of [[Tōbokuin]], in consultation with two other senior monks of [[Kōfuku-ji]] (Jishin of Daijōin and Hanken of Sanzōin).
Mototada copied out scrolls 1-5, 9-13 and 16; the eldest son, [[:en:Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|Fuyuhira]], copied out scrolls 6-8; the second son, Fuyumoto, copied out scrolls 14, 15, 18 and 19; and the fourth son, Ryōshin, copied out scrolls 17 and 18. Takashina Takekane, who was the director of the imperial painting bureau (edokoro), created the illustrations. Following the completion of the work, the Minister of the Left ([[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]), [[:en:Saionji family|Saionji]] Kinhira (who was also a brother to Kakuen), dedicated it to the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] and neighboring [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist temple]], [[:en:Kōfukuji|Kōfukuji]], in order to honor the deities and thank them for honoring his home.
When the scrolls were first completed and kept at the Kasuga shrine, the scrolls were carefully protected—with no shrine priest or Kōfukuji monk less than 40 years of age able to view the scrolls. Moreover, if the scrolls were required at Kōfukuji, it was only the Tōbokuin sanctuary that was permitted to receive the scrolls. However, for a while, during the late 18th Century, the scrolls became less carefully looked after. The scrolls were most likely treated more casually because of the decline suffered by Kasuga and Kōfukuji at the time.
In order to make Kasuga Gongen genki e more accessible to the public, copies of the work began to appear during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. However, permission from the head of the [[Fujiwara clan]] was needed before any copies were allowed. Currently there are 6 known copies to be made: one owned by the Kajūji family, the [[:en:Yōmei Bunko|Yōmei Bunko]] copy (Yōmei Bunko-bon), the Kasuga copy (Kasuga-bon/Kuwana-bon), the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] copy (Tōkyō Kokuritsu Hakubutsukan-bon) no. 1, the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]] copy (Kokuritsu Kokkai Toshokan-bon), and the Tokyo National Museum copy no. 2 (3). The original work is of extremely high quality and is kept by the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial household]].
==English Translation== [[:en:Royall Tyler (academic)|Royall Tyler]] has completed a study and complete English translation of Kasuga Gongen genki e, under the title “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity”, which was published in 1990. Within his translation, Royall Tyler refers to Kasuga Gongen genki as “the Genki”. The first part of the book gives background information on the Kasuga shrine, Kōfukuji, the Kasuga cult and the religious background and context in which the original work was created. The second part of the book gives a complete translation of all 20 scrolls, with annotations to assist the reader in understanding the work.
==See also== *[[:en:Japanese painting|Japanese painting]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]]
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Kasuga Gongen Reigen VOL2s1.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s3.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s2.jpg Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s4.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s6.jpg Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s5.JPG </gallery>
==External links== *[http://mahoroba.lib.nara-wu.ac.jp/y04/tyler/t01.html About the work] by [[:en:Royall Tyler|Royall Tyler]] *[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2499 Book review "The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity"]

Currently there are 6 known copies to be made: one owned by the Kajūji family, the [[:en:Yōmei Bunko|Yōmei Bunko]] copy (Yōmei Bunko-bon), the Kasuga copy (Kasuga-bon/Kuwana-bon), the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] copy (Tōkyō Kokuritsu Hakubutsukan-bon) no. 1, the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]] copy (Kokuritsu Kokkai Toshokan-bon), and the Tokyo National Museum copy no. 2 (3). The original work is of extremely high quality and is kept by the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial household]].
==English Translation== [[:en:Royall Tyler (academic)|Royall Tyler]] has completed a study and complete English translation of Kasuga Gongen genki e, under the title “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity”, which was published in 1990. Within his translation, Royall Tyler refers to Kasuga Gongen genki as “the Genki”. The first part of the book gives background information on the Kasuga shrine, Kōfukuji, the Kasuga cult and the religious background and context in which the original work was created. The second part of the book gives a complete translation of all 20 scrolls, with annotations to assist the reader in understanding the work.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] {{Nihongo|'''Kasuga Gongen Genki E'''|春日権現験記絵}} is a set of painted handscrolls ([[:en:Emakimono|Emakimono]]) that was produced during the early 14th Century ([[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]) of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], by members of the Fujiwara clan. The work was created in order to honor the deities of Kasuga, with the stories revolving around the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga shrine]] and [[Kōfuku-ji|Kōfukuji temple]], located in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. The colourful silk work was made with silver and gold paints and consists of 20 scrolls with 93 sections of text and illustrations, which was completed in 1309.
[[File:Kasuga gongen genki-e, Takashina Takakane, 1-3 (part 1).JPG|thumb|Kasuga Gongen Genki E]]
==Meaning of the name== The term “Gongen” means [[:en:Avatar|Avatar]] or [[Kami|Deity]], while the term “genki” in the title is actually short for “reigenki”; reigenki can be translated into “chronicles of marvelous and supernatural events”. Taken together, the title has been translated into “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity” or “Illustrated Tales of the Miracles of the Avatars of Kasuga” or the equivalent. And as the title suggests, the work focuses on miraculous events involving the Kasuga Shrine and Buddhist/Shinto deities.
==Authorship and history== The former regent ([[Sesshō and Kampaku|kanpaku]]), [[:en:Takatsukasa Mototada|Takatsukasa (Fujiwara) Mototada]], and his three sons wrote the original text, based on stories compiled by a monk named Kakuen of [[Tōbokuin]], in consultation with two other senior monks of [[Kōfuku-ji]] (Jishin of Daijōin and Hanken of Sanzōin).
Mototada copied out scrolls 1-5, 9-13 and 16; the eldest son, [[:en:Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|Fuyuhira]], copied out scrolls 6-8; the second son, Fuyumoto, copied out scrolls 14, 15, 18 and 19; and the fourth son, Ryōshin, copied out scrolls 17 and 18. Takashina Takekane, who was the director of the imperial painting bureau (edokoro), created the illustrations. Following the completion of the work, the Minister of the Left ([[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]), [[:en:Saionji family|Saionji]] Kinhira (who was also a brother to Kakuen), dedicated it to the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] and neighboring [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist temple]], [[:en:Kōfukuji|Kōfukuji]], in order to honor the deities and thank them for honoring his home.
When the scrolls were first completed and kept at the Kasuga shrine, the scrolls were carefully protected—with no shrine priest or Kōfukuji monk less than 40 years of age able to view the scrolls. Moreover, if the scrolls were required at Kōfukuji, it was only the Tōbokuin sanctuary that was permitted to receive the scrolls. However, for a while, during the late 18th Century, the scrolls became less carefully looked after. The scrolls were most likely treated more casually because of the decline suffered by Kasuga and Kōfukuji at the time.
In order to make Kasuga Gongen genki e more accessible to the public, copies of the work began to appear during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. However, permission from the head of the [[Fujiwara clan]] was needed before any copies were allowed. Currently there are 6 known copies to be made: one owned by the Kajūji family, the [[:en:Yōmei Bunko|Yōmei Bunko]] copy (Yōmei Bunko-bon), the Kasuga copy (Kasuga-bon/Kuwana-bon), the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] copy (Tōkyō Kokuritsu Hakubutsukan-bon) no. 1, the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]] copy (Kokuritsu Kokkai Toshokan-bon), and the Tokyo National Museum copy no. 2 (3). The original work is of extremely high quality and is kept by the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial household]].
==English Translation== [[:en:Royall Tyler (academic)|Royall Tyler]] has completed a study and complete English translation of Kasuga Gongen genki e, under the title “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity”, which was published in 1990. The first part of the book gives background information on the Kasuga shrine, Kōfukuji, the Kasuga cult and the religious background and context in which the original work was created. The second part of the book gives a complete translation of all 20 scrolls, with annotations to assist the reader in understanding the work.
==See also== *[[:en:Japanese painting|Japanese painting]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] {{Nihongo|'''Kasuga Gongen Genki E'''|春日権現験記絵}} is a set of painted handscrolls ([[:en:Emakimono|Emakimono]]) that was produced during the early 14th Century ([[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]) of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], by members of the Fujiwara clan. The work was created in order to honor the deities of Kasuga, with the stories revolving around the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga shrine]] and [[Kōfuku-ji|Kōfukuji temple]], located in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. The colourful silk work was made with silver and gold paints and consists of 20 scrolls with 93 sections of text and illustrations, which was completed in 1309.
[[File:Kasuga gongen genki-e, Takashina Takakane, 1-3 (part 1).JPG|thumb|Kasuga Gongen Genki E]]
==Meaning of the name== The term “Gongen” means [[:en:Avatar|Avatar]] or [[Kami|Deity]], while the term “genki” in the title is actually short for “reigenki”; reigenki can be translated into “chronicles of marvelous and supernatural events”. Taken together, the title has been translated into “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity” or “Illustrated Tales of the Miracles of the Avatars of Kasuga” or the equivalent. And as the title suggests, the work focuses on miraculous events involving the Kasuga Shrine and Buddhist/Shinto deities.
==Authorship and history== The former regent ([[Sesshō and Kampaku|kanpaku]]), [[:en:Takatsukasa Mototada|Takatsukasa (Fujiwara) Mototada]], and his three sons wrote the original text, based on stories compiled by a monk named Kakuen of [[Tōbokuin]], in consultation with two other senior monks of [[Kōfuku-ji]] (Jishin of Daijōin and Hanken of Sanzōin).
Mototada copied out scrolls 1-5, 9-13 and 16; the eldest son, [[:en:Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|Fuyuhira]], copied out scrolls 6-8; the second son, Fuyumoto, copied out scrolls 14, 15, 18 and 19; and the fourth son, Ryōshin, copied out scrolls 17 and 18. Takashina Takekane, who was the director of the imperial painting bureau (edokoro), created the illustrations. Following the completion of the work, the Minister of the Left ([[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]), [[:en:Saionji family|Saionji]] Kinhira (who was also a brother to Kakuen), dedicated it to the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] and neighboring [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist temple]], [[:en:Kōfukuji|Kōfukuji]], in order to honor the deities and thank them for honoring his home.
When the scrolls were first completed and kept at the Kasuga shrine, the scrolls were carefully protected—with no shrine priest or Kōfukuji monk less than 40 years of age able to view the scrolls. Moreover, if the scrolls were required at Kōfukuji, it was only the Tōbokuin sanctuary that was permitted to receive the scrolls. However, for a while, during the late 18th Century, the scrolls became less carefully looked after. The scrolls were most likely treated more casually because of the decline suffered by Kasuga and Kōfukuji at the time.
In order to make Kasuga Gongen genki e more accessible to the public, copies of the work began to appear during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. However, permission from the head of the [[Fujiwara clan]] was needed before any copies were allowed. Currently there are 6 known copies to be made: one owned by the Kajūji family, the [[:en:Yōmei Bunko|Yōmei Bunko]] copy (Yōmei Bunko-bon), the Kasuga copy (Kasuga-bon/Kuwana-bon), the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] copy (Tōkyō Kokuritsu Hakubutsukan-bon) no. 1, the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]] copy (Kokuritsu Kokkai Toshokan-bon), and the Tokyo National Museum copy no. 2 (3). The original work is of extremely high quality and is kept by the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial household]].
==English Translation== [[:en:Royall Tyler (academic)|Royall Tyler]] has completed a study and complete English translation of Kasuga Gongen genki e, under the title “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity”, which was published in 1990. Within his translation, Royall Tyler refers to Kasuga Gongen genki as “the Genki”. The first part of the book gives background information on the Kasuga shrine, Kōfukuji, the Kasuga cult and the religious background and context in which the original work was created. The second part of the book gives a complete translation of all 20 scrolls, with annotations to assist the reader in understanding the work.
==See also== *[[:en:Japanese painting|Japanese painting]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]]
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Kasuga Gongen Reigen VOL2s1.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s3.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s2.jpg Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s4.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s6.jpg Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s5.JPG </gallery>
==External links== *[http://mahoroba.lib.nara-wu.ac.jp/y04/tyler/t01.html About the work] by [[:en:Royall Tyler|Royall Tyler]] *[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2499 Book review "The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity"]
{{Authority control}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] {{Nihongo|'''Kasuga Gongen Genki E'''|春日権現験記絵}} is a set of painted handscrolls ([[:en:Emakimono|Emakimono]]) that was produced during the early 14th Century ([[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]) of [[:en:Japan|Japan]], by members of the Fujiwara clan. The work was created in order to honor the deities of Kasuga, with the stories revolving around the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga shrine]] and [[Kōfuku-ji|Kōfukuji temple]], located in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. The colourful silk work was made with silver and gold paints and consists of 20 scrolls with 93 sections of text and illustrations, which was completed in 1309.
[[File:Kasuga gongen genki-e, Takashina Takakane, 1-3 (part 1).JPG|thumb|Kasuga Gongen Genki E]]
==Meaning of the name== The term “Gongen” means [[:en:Avatar|Avatar]] or [[Kami|Deity]], while the term “genki” in the title is actually short for “reigenki”; reigenki can be translated into “chronicles of marvelous and supernatural events”. Taken together, the title has been translated into “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity” or “Illustrated Tales of the Miracles of the Avatars of Kasuga” or the equivalent. And as the title suggests, the work focuses on miraculous events involving the Kasuga Shrine and Buddhist/Shinto deities.
==Authorship and history== The former regent ([[Sesshō and Kampaku|kanpaku]]), [[:en:Takatsukasa Mototada|Takatsukasa (Fujiwara) Mototada]], and his three sons wrote the original text, based on stories compiled by a monk named Kakuen of [[Tōbokuin]], in consultation with two other senior monks of [[Kōfuku-ji]] (Jishin of Daijōin and Hanken of Sanzōin).
Mototada copied out scrolls 1-5, 9-13 and 16; the eldest son, [[:en:Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|Fuyuhira]], copied out scrolls 6-8; the second son, Fuyumoto, copied out scrolls 14, 15, 18 and 19; and the fourth son, Ryōshin, copied out scrolls 17 and 18. Takashina Takekane, who was the director of the imperial painting bureau (edokoro), created the illustrations. Following the completion of the work, the Minister of the Left ([[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]), [[:en:Saionji family|Saionji]] Kinhira (who was also a brother to Kakuen), dedicated it to the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] and neighboring [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist temple]], [[:en:Kōfukuji|Kōfukuji]], in order to honor the deities and thank them for honoring his home.
When the scrolls were first completed and kept at the Kasuga shrine, the scrolls were carefully protected—with no shrine priest or Kōfukuji monk less than 40 years of age able to view the scrolls. Moreover, if the scrolls were required at Kōfukuji, it was only the Tōbokuin sanctuary that was permitted to receive the scrolls. However, for a while, during the late 18th Century, the scrolls became less carefully looked after. The scrolls were most likely treated more casually because of the decline suffered by Kasuga and Kōfukuji at the time.
In order to make Kasuga Gongen genki e more accessible to the public, copies of the work began to appear during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. However, permission from the head of the [[Fujiwara clan]] was needed before any copies were allowed. Currently there are 6 known copies to be made: one owned by the Kajūji family, the [[:en:Yōmei Bunko|Yōmei Bunko]] copy (Yōmei Bunko-bon), the Kasuga copy (Kasuga-bon/Kuwana-bon), the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] copy (Tōkyō Kokuritsu Hakubutsukan-bon) no. 1, the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]] copy (Kokuritsu Kokkai Toshokan-bon), and the Tokyo National Museum copy no. 2 (3). The original work is of extremely high quality and is kept by the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial household]].
==English Translation== [[:en:Royall Tyler (academic)|Royall Tyler]] has completed a study and complete English translation of Kasuga Gongen genki e, under the title “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity”, which was published in 1990. Within his translation, Royall Tyler refers to Kasuga Gongen genki as “the Genki”. The first part of the book gives background information on the Kasuga shrine, Kōfukuji, the Kasuga cult and the religious background and context in which the original work was created. The second part of the book gives a complete translation of all 20 scrolls, with annotations to assist the reader in understanding the work.
==See also== *[[:en:Japanese painting|Japanese painting]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]]
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Kasuga Gongen Reigen VOL2s1.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s3.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s2.jpg Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s4.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s6.jpg Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s5.JPG </gallery>
==External links== *[http://mahoroba.lib.nara-wu.ac.jp/y04/tyler/t01.html About the work] by [[:en:Royall Tyler|Royall Tyler]] *[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2499 Book review "The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity"]
{{Authority control}}

==External links== *[http://mahoroba.lib.nara-wu.ac.jp/y04/tyler/t01.html About the work] by {{ill|Royall Tyler|en|Royall Tyler|WD=Q2171170|lt=Royall Tyler}} *[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2499 Book review "The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity"]

[[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]]
{{wikidata link|Q6374869}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Emakimono]] [[Category:Gongen]] [[Category:History of art in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese chronicles]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:Wikidata has English label]]" to "[[Category:qq]]"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] {{Nihongo|'''Kasuga Gongen Genki E'''|春日権現験記絵}} is a set of painted handscrolls ({{ill|Emakimono|en|Emakimono|WD=Q1190781|lt=Emakimono}}) that was produced during the early 14th Century ({{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}) of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, by members of the Fujiwara clan. The work was created in order to honor the deities of Kasuga, with the stories revolving around the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga shrine]] and [[Kōfuku-ji|Kōfukuji temple]], located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara}}. The colourful silk work was made with silver and gold paints and consists of 20 scrolls with 93 sections of text and illustrations, which was completed in 1309.
[[File:Kasuga gongen genki-e, Takashina Takakane, 1-3 (part 1).JPG|thumb|Kasuga Gongen Genki E]]
==Meaning of the name== The term “Gongen” means {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|WD=Q83090|lt=Avatar}} or [[Kami|Deity]], while the term “genki” in the title is actually short for “reigenki”; reigenki can be translated into “chronicles of marvelous and supernatural events”. Taken together, the title has been translated into “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity” or “Illustrated Tales of the Miracles of the Avatars of Kasuga” or the equivalent. And as the title suggests, the work focuses on miraculous events involving the Kasuga Shrine and Buddhist/Shinto deities.
==Authorship and history== The former regent ([[Sesshō and Kampaku|kanpaku]]), {{ill|Takatsukasa Mototada|en|Takatsukasa Mototada|WD=Q955290|lt=Takatsukasa (Fujiwara) Mototada}}, and his three sons wrote the original text, based on stories compiled by a monk named Kakuen of [[Tōbokuin]], in consultation with two other senior monks of [[Kōfuku-ji]] (Jishin of Daijōin and Hanken of Sanzōin).
Mototada copied out scrolls 1-5, 9-13 and 16; the eldest son, {{ill|Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|en|Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|WD=Q3514218|lt=Fuyuhira}}, copied out scrolls 6-8; the second son, Fuyumoto, copied out scrolls 14, 15, 18 and 19; and the fourth son, Ryōshin, copied out scrolls 17 and 18. Takashina Takekane, who was the director of the imperial painting bureau (edokoro), created the illustrations. Following the completion of the work, the Minister of the Left ([[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]), {{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji}} Kinhira (who was also a brother to Kakuen), dedicated it to the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] and neighboring {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist temple}}, [[:en:Kōfukuji|Kōfukuji]], in order to honor the deities and thank them for honoring his home.
When the scrolls were first completed and kept at the Kasuga shrine, the scrolls were carefully protected—with no shrine priest or Kōfukuji monk less than 40 years of age able to view the scrolls. Moreover, if the scrolls were required at Kōfukuji, it was only the Tōbokuin sanctuary that was permitted to receive the scrolls. However, for a while, during the late 18th Century, the scrolls became less carefully looked after. The scrolls were most likely treated more casually because of the decline suffered by Kasuga and Kōfukuji at the time.
In order to make Kasuga Gongen genki e more accessible to the public, copies of the work began to appear during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. However, permission from the head of the [[Fujiwara clan]] was needed before any copies were allowed. Currently there are 6 known copies to be made: one owned by the Kajūji family, the {{ill|Yōmei Bunko|en|Yōmei Bunko|WD=Q11658779|lt=Yōmei Bunko}} copy (Yōmei Bunko-bon), the Kasuga copy (Kasuga-bon/Kuwana-bon), the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}} copy (Tōkyō Kokuritsu Hakubutsukan-bon) no. 1, the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library}} copy (Kokuritsu Kokkai Toshokan-bon), and the Tokyo National Museum copy no. 2 (3). The original work is of extremely high quality and is kept by the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial household}}.
==English Translation== {{ill|Royall Tyler (academic)|en|Royall Tyler (academic)|WD=Q3826510|lt=Royall Tyler}} has completed a study and complete English translation of Kasuga Gongen genki e, under the title “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity”, which was published in 1990. Within his translation, Royall Tyler refers to Kasuga Gongen genki as “the Genki”. The first part of the book gives background information on the Kasuga shrine, Kōfukuji, the Kasuga cult and the religious background and context in which the original work was created. The second part of the book gives a complete translation of all 20 scrolls, with annotations to assist the reader in understanding the work.
==See also== *{{ill|Japanese painting|en|Japanese painting|WD=Q1683295|lt=Japanese painting}} *{{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}}
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Kasuga Gongen Reigen VOL2s1.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s3.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s2.jpg Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s4.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s6.jpg Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s5.JPG </gallery>
==External links== *[http://mahoroba.lib.nara-wu.ac.jp/y04/tyler/t01.html About the work] by {{ill|Royall Tyler|en|Royall Tyler|WD=Q2171170|lt=Royall Tyler}} *[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2499 Book review "The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity"]
{{Authority control}}

Following the completion of the work, the Minister of the Left ([[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]), {{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji}} Kinhira (who was also a brother to Kakuen), dedicated it to the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] and neighboring {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist temple}}, [[:en:Kōfukuji|Kōfukuji]], in order to honor the deities and thank them for honoring his home.
When the scrolls were first completed and kept at the Kasuga shrine, the scrolls were carefully protected—with no shrine priest or Kōfukuji monk less than 40 years of age able to view the scrolls. Moreover, if the scrolls were required at Kōfukuji, it was only the Tōbokuin sanctuary that was permitted to receive the scrolls. However, for a while, during the late 18th Century, the scrolls became less carefully looked after. The scrolls were most likely treated more casually because of the decline suffered by Kasuga and Kōfukuji at the time.
In order to make Kasuga Gongen genki e more accessible to the public, copies of the work began to appear during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. However, permission from the head of the [[Fujiwara clan]] was needed before any copies were allowed. Currently there are 6 known copies to be made: one owned by the Kajūji family, the {{ill|Yōmei Bunko|en|Yōmei Bunko|WD=Q11658779|lt=Yōmei Bunko}} copy (Yōmei Bunko-bon), the Kasuga copy (Kasuga-bon/Kuwana-bon), the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}} copy (Tōkyō Kokuritsu Hakubutsukan-bon) no. 1, the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library}} copy (Kokuritsu Kokkai Toshokan-bon), and the Tokyo National Museum copy no. 2 (3). The original work is of extremely high quality and is kept by the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial household}}.
==English Translation== {{ill|Royall Tyler (academic)|en|Royall Tyler (academic)|WD=Q3826510|lt=Royall Tyler}} has completed a study and complete English translation of Kasuga Gongen genki e, under the title “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity”, which was published in 1990. Within his translation, Royall Tyler refers to Kasuga Gongen genki as “the Genki”. The first part of the book gives background information on the Kasuga shrine, Kōfukuji, the Kasuga cult and the religious background and context in which the original work was created. The second part of the book gives a complete translation of all 20 scrolls, with annotations to assist the reader in understanding the work.
==See also== *{{ill|Japanese painting|en|Japanese painting|WD=Q1683295|lt=Japanese painting}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}}

==External links== *[http://mahoroba.lib.nara-wu.ac.jp/y04/tyler/t01.html About the work] by {{ill|Royall Tyler|en|Royall Tyler|WD=Q2171170|lt=Royall Tyler}} *[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2499 Book review "The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity"]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] {{Nihongo|'''Kasuga Gongen Genki E'''|春日権現験記絵}} is a set of painted handscrolls ({{ill|Emakimono|en|Emakimono|WD=Q1190781|lt=Emakimono}}) that was produced during the early 14th Century ({{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}) of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, by members of the Fujiwara clan. The work was created in order to honor the deities of Kasuga, with the stories revolving around the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga shrine]] and [[Kōfuku-ji|Kōfukuji temple]], located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara}}. The colourful silk work was made with silver and gold paints and consists of 20 scrolls with 93 sections of text and illustrations, which was completed in 1309.
[[File:Kasuga gongen genki-e, Takashina Takakane, 1-3 (part 1).JPG|thumb|Kasuga Gongen Genki E]]
==Meaning of the name== The term “Gongen” means {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|WD=Q83090|lt=Avatar}} or [[Kami|Deity]], while the term “genki” in the title is actually short for “reigenki”; reigenki can be translated into “chronicles of marvelous and supernatural events”. Taken together, the title has been translated into “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity” or “Illustrated Tales of the Miracles of the Avatars of Kasuga” or the equivalent. And as the title suggests, the work focuses on miraculous events involving the Kasuga Shrine and Buddhist/Shinto deities.
==Authorship and history== The former regent ([[Sesshō and Kampaku|kanpaku]]), {{ill|Takatsukasa Mototada|en|Takatsukasa Mototada|WD=Q955290|lt=Takatsukasa (Fujiwara) Mototada}}, and his three sons wrote the original text, based on stories compiled by a monk named Kakuen of [[Tōbokuin]], in consultation with two other senior monks of [[Kōfuku-ji]] (Jishin of Daijōin and Hanken of Sanzōin).
Mototada copied out scrolls 1-5, 9-13 and 16; the eldest son, {{ill|Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|en|Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|WD=Q3514218|lt=Fuyuhira}}, copied out scrolls 6-8; the second son, Fuyumoto, copied out scrolls 14, 15, 18 and 19; and the fourth son, Ryōshin, copied out scrolls 17 and 18. Takashina Takekane, who was the director of the imperial painting bureau (edokoro), created the illustrations. Following the completion of the work, the Minister of the Left ([[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]), {{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji}} Kinhira (who was also a brother to Kakuen), dedicated it to the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] and neighboring {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist temple}}, [[:en:Kōfukuji|Kōfukuji]], in order to honor the deities and thank them for honoring his home.
When the scrolls were first completed and kept at the Kasuga shrine, the scrolls were carefully protected—with no shrine priest or Kōfukuji monk less than 40 years of age able to view the scrolls. Moreover, if the scrolls were required at Kōfukuji, it was only the Tōbokuin sanctuary that was permitted to receive the scrolls. However, for a while, during the late 18th Century, the scrolls became less carefully looked after. The scrolls were most likely treated more casually because of the decline suffered by Kasuga and Kōfukuji at the time.
In order to make Kasuga Gongen genki e more accessible to the public, copies of the work began to appear during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. However, permission from the head of the [[Fujiwara clan]] was needed before any copies were allowed. Currently there are 6 known copies to be made: one owned by the Kajūji family, the {{ill|Yōmei Bunko|en|Yōmei Bunko|WD=Q11658779|lt=Yōmei Bunko}} copy (Yōmei Bunko-bon), the Kasuga copy (Kasuga-bon/Kuwana-bon), the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}} copy (Tōkyō Kokuritsu Hakubutsukan-bon) no. 1, the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library}} copy (Kokuritsu Kokkai Toshokan-bon), and the Tokyo National Museum copy no. 2 (3). The original work is of extremely high quality and is kept by the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial household}}.
==English Translation== {{ill|Royall Tyler (academic)|en|Royall Tyler (academic)|WD=Q3826510|lt=Royall Tyler}} has completed a study and complete English translation of Kasuga Gongen genki e, under the title “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity”, which was published in 1990. Within his translation, Royall Tyler refers to Kasuga Gongen genki as “the Genki”. The first part of the book gives background information on the Kasuga shrine, Kōfukuji, the Kasuga cult and the religious background and context in which the original work was created. The second part of the book gives a complete translation of all 20 scrolls, with annotations to assist the reader in understanding the work.
==See also== *{{ill|Japanese painting|en|Japanese painting|WD=Q1683295|lt=Japanese painting}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}}
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Kasuga Gongen Reigen VOL2s1.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s3.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s2.jpg Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s4.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s6.jpg Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s5.JPG </gallery>
==External links== *[http://mahoroba.lib.nara-wu.ac.jp/y04/tyler/t01.html About the work] by {{ill|Royall Tyler|en|Royall Tyler|WD=Q2171170|lt=Royall Tyler}} *[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2499 Book review "The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity"]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] {{Nihongo|'''Kasuga Gongen Genki E'''|春日権現験記絵}} is a set of painted handscrolls ({{ill|Emakimono|en|Emakimono|WD=Q1190781|lt=Emakimono|1=Emakimono}}) that was produced during the early 14th Century ({{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}) of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, by members of the Fujiwara clan. The work was created in order to honor the deities of Kasuga, with the stories revolving around the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga shrine]] and [[Kōfuku-ji|Kōfukuji temple]], located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The colourful silk work was made with silver and gold paints and consists of 20 scrolls with 93 sections of text and illustrations, which was completed in 1309.
[[File:Kasuga gongen genki-e, Takashina Takakane, 1-3 (part 1).JPG|thumb|Kasuga Gongen Genki E]]
==Meaning of the name== The term “Gongen” means {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|WD=Q83090|lt=Avatar|1=Avatar}} or [[Kami|Deity]], while the term “genki” in the title is actually short for “reigenki”; reigenki can be translated into “chronicles of marvelous and supernatural events”. Taken together, the title has been translated into “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity” or “Illustrated Tales of the Miracles of the Avatars of Kasuga” or the equivalent. And as the title suggests, the work focuses on miraculous events involving the Kasuga Shrine and Buddhist/Shinto deities.
==Authorship and history== The former regent ([[Sesshō and Kampaku|kanpaku]]), {{ill|Takatsukasa Mototada|en|Takatsukasa Mototada|WD=Q955290|lt=Takatsukasa (Fujiwara) Mototada|1=Takatsukasa Mototada}}, and his three sons wrote the original text, based on stories compiled by a monk named Kakuen of [[Tōbokuin]], in consultation with two other senior monks of [[Kōfuku-ji]] (Jishin of Daijōin and Hanken of Sanzōin).
Mototada copied out scrolls 1-5, 9-13 and 16; the eldest son, {{ill|Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|en|Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|WD=Q3514218|lt=Fuyuhira|1=Takatsukasa Fuyuhira}}, copied out scrolls 6-8; the second son, Fuyumoto, copied out scrolls 14, 15, 18 and 19; and the fourth son, Ryōshin, copied out scrolls 17 and 18. Takashina Takekane, who was the director of the imperial painting bureau (edokoro), created the illustrations. Following the completion of the work, the Minister of the Left ([[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]), {{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji|1=Saionji family}} Kinhira (who was also a brother to Kakuen), dedicated it to the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] and neighboring {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhism}}, [[:en:Kōfukuji|Kōfukuji]], in order to honor the deities and thank them for honoring his home.
When the scrolls were first completed and kept at the Kasuga shrine, the scrolls were carefully protected—with no shrine priest or Kōfukuji monk less than 40 years of age able to view the scrolls. Moreover, if the scrolls were required at Kōfukuji, it was only the Tōbokuin sanctuary that was permitted to receive the scrolls. However, for a while, during the late 18th Century, the scrolls became less carefully looked after. The scrolls were most likely treated more casually because of the decline suffered by Kasuga and Kōfukuji at the time.
In order to make Kasuga Gongen genki e more accessible to the public, copies of the work began to appear during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. However, permission from the head of the [[Fujiwara clan]] was needed before any copies were allowed. Currently there are 6 known copies to be made: one owned by the Kajūji family, the {{ill|Yōmei Bunko|en|Yōmei Bunko|WD=Q11658779|lt=Yōmei Bunko|1=Yōmei Bunko}} copy (Yōmei Bunko-bon), the Kasuga copy (Kasuga-bon/Kuwana-bon), the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}} copy (Tōkyō Kokuritsu Hakubutsukan-bon) no. 1, the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library|1=National Diet Library}} copy (Kokuritsu Kokkai Toshokan-bon), and the Tokyo National Museum copy no. 2 (3). The original work is of extremely high quality and is kept by the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial household|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.
==English Translation== {{ill|Royall Tyler (academic)|en|Royall Tyler (academic)|WD=Q3826510|lt=Royall Tyler|1=Royall Tyler (academic)}} has completed a study and complete English translation of Kasuga Gongen genki e, under the title “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity”, which was published in 1990. Within his translation, Royall Tyler refers to Kasuga Gongen genki as “the Genki”. The first part of the book gives background information on the Kasuga shrine, Kōfukuji, the Kasuga cult and the religious background and context in which the original work was created. The second part of the book gives a complete translation of all 20 scrolls, with annotations to assist the reader in understanding the work.
==See also== *{{ill|Japanese painting|en|Japanese painting|WD=Q1683295|lt=Japanese painting|1=Japanese painting}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}}

Following the completion of the work, the Minister of the Left ([[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]), {{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji|1=Saionji family}} Kinhira (who was also a brother to Kakuen), dedicated it to the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] and neighboring {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhism}}, [[:en:Kōfukuji|Kōfukuji]], in order to honor the deities and thank them for honoring his home.
When the scrolls were first completed and kept at the Kasuga shrine, the scrolls were carefully protected—with no shrine priest or Kōfukuji monk less than 40 years of age able to view the scrolls. Moreover, if the scrolls were required at Kōfukuji, it was only the Tōbokuin sanctuary that was permitted to receive the scrolls. However, for a while, during the late 18th Century, the scrolls became less carefully looked after. The scrolls were most likely treated more casually because of the decline suffered by Kasuga and Kōfukuji at the time.
In order to make Kasuga Gongen genki e more accessible to the public, copies of the work began to appear during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. However, permission from the head of the [[Fujiwara clan]] was needed before any copies were allowed. Currently there are 6 known copies to be made: one owned by the Kajūji family, the {{ill|Yōmei Bunko|en|Yōmei Bunko|WD=Q11658779|lt=Yōmei Bunko|1=Yōmei Bunko}} copy (Yōmei Bunko-bon), the Kasuga copy (Kasuga-bon/Kuwana-bon), the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}} copy (Tōkyō Kokuritsu Hakubutsukan-bon) no. 1, the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library|1=National Diet Library}} copy (Kokuritsu Kokkai Toshokan-bon), and the Tokyo National Museum copy no. 2 (3). The original work is of extremely high quality and is kept by the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial household|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.
==English Translation== {{ill|Royall Tyler (academic)|en|Royall Tyler (academic)|WD=Q3826510|lt=Royall Tyler|1=Royall Tyler (academic)}} has completed a study and complete English translation of Kasuga Gongen genki e, under the title “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity”, which was published in 1990. Within his translation, Royall Tyler refers to Kasuga Gongen genki as “the Genki”. The first part of the book gives background information on the Kasuga shrine, Kōfukuji, the Kasuga cult and the religious background and context in which the original work was created. The second part of the book gives a complete translation of all 20 scrolls, with annotations to assist the reader in understanding the work.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[en:Kasuga Gongen Genki E]][[fr:Kasuga gongen genki-e]][[ja:春日権現験記]] {{Nihongo|'''Kasuga Gongen Genki E'''|春日権現験記絵}} is a set of painted handscrolls ({{ill|Emakimono|en|Emakimono|WD=Q1190781|lt=Emakimono|1=Emakimono}}) that was produced during the early 14th Century ({{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}) of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, by members of the Fujiwara clan. The work was created in order to honor the deities of Kasuga, with the stories revolving around the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga shrine]] and [[Kōfuku-ji|Kōfukuji temple]], located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The colourful silk work was made with silver and gold paints and consists of 20 scrolls with 93 sections of text and illustrations, which was completed in 1309.
[[File:Kasuga gongen genki-e, Takashina Takakane, 1-3 (part 1).JPG|thumb|Kasuga Gongen Genki E]]
==Meaning of the name== The term “Gongen” means {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|WD=Q83090|lt=Avatar|1=Avatar}} or [[Kami|Deity]], while the term “genki” in the title is actually short for “reigenki”; reigenki can be translated into “chronicles of marvelous and supernatural events”. Taken together, the title has been translated into “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity” or “Illustrated Tales of the Miracles of the Avatars of Kasuga” or the equivalent. And as the title suggests, the work focuses on miraculous events involving the Kasuga Shrine and Buddhist/Shinto deities.
==Authorship and history== The former regent ([[Sesshō and Kampaku|kanpaku]]), {{ill|Takatsukasa Mototada|en|Takatsukasa Mototada|WD=Q955290|lt=Takatsukasa (Fujiwara) Mototada|1=Takatsukasa Mototada}}, and his three sons wrote the original text, based on stories compiled by a monk named Kakuen of [[Tōbokuin]], in consultation with two other senior monks of [[Kōfuku-ji]] (Jishin of Daijōin and Hanken of Sanzōin).
Mototada copied out scrolls 1-5, 9-13 and 16; the eldest son, {{ill|Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|en|Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|WD=Q3514218|lt=Fuyuhira|1=Takatsukasa Fuyuhira}}, copied out scrolls 6-8; the second son, Fuyumoto, copied out scrolls 14, 15, 18 and 19; and the fourth son, Ryōshin, copied out scrolls 17 and 18. Takashina Takekane, who was the director of the imperial painting bureau (edokoro), created the illustrations. Following the completion of the work, the Minister of the Left ([[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]), {{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|WD=Q7402472|lt=Saionji|1=Saionji family}} Kinhira (who was also a brother to Kakuen), dedicated it to the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] and neighboring {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhism}}, [[:en:Kōfukuji|Kōfukuji]], in order to honor the deities and thank them for honoring his home.
When the scrolls were first completed and kept at the Kasuga shrine, the scrolls were carefully protected—with no shrine priest or Kōfukuji monk less than 40 years of age able to view the scrolls. Moreover, if the scrolls were required at Kōfukuji, it was only the Tōbokuin sanctuary that was permitted to receive the scrolls. However, for a while, during the late 18th Century, the scrolls became less carefully looked after. The scrolls were most likely treated more casually because of the decline suffered by Kasuga and Kōfukuji at the time.
In order to make Kasuga Gongen genki e more accessible to the public, copies of the work began to appear during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. However, permission from the head of the [[Fujiwara clan]] was needed before any copies were allowed. Currently there are 6 known copies to be made: one owned by the Kajūji family, the {{ill|Yōmei Bunko|en|Yōmei Bunko|WD=Q11658779|lt=Yōmei Bunko|1=Yōmei Bunko}} copy (Yōmei Bunko-bon), the Kasuga copy (Kasuga-bon/Kuwana-bon), the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}} copy (Tōkyō Kokuritsu Hakubutsukan-bon) no. 1, the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library|1=National Diet Library}} copy (Kokuritsu Kokkai Toshokan-bon), and the Tokyo National Museum copy no. 2 (3). The original work is of extremely high quality and is kept by the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial household|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.
==English Translation== {{ill|Royall Tyler (academic)|en|Royall Tyler (academic)|WD=Q3826510|lt=Royall Tyler|1=Royall Tyler (academic)}} has completed a study and complete English translation of Kasuga Gongen genki e, under the title “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity”, which was published in 1990. Within his translation, Royall Tyler refers to Kasuga Gongen genki as “the Genki”. The first part of the book gives background information on the Kasuga shrine, Kōfukuji, the Kasuga cult and the religious background and context in which the original work was created. The second part of the book gives a complete translation of all 20 scrolls, with annotations to assist the reader in understanding the work.
==See also== *{{ill|Japanese painting|en|Japanese painting|WD=Q1683295|lt=Japanese painting|1=Japanese painting}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}}
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Kasuga Gongen Reigen VOL2s1.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s3.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s2.jpg Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s4.JPG Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s6.jpg Image:Kasuga Gongen vol2s5.JPG </gallery>
==External links== *[http://mahoroba.lib.nara-wu.ac.jp/y04/tyler/t01.html About the work] by {{ill|Royall Tyler|en|Royall Tyler|WD=Q2171170|lt=Royall Tyler|1=Royall Tyler}} *[https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2499 Book review "The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity"]

The second part of the book gives a complete translation of all 20 scrolls, with annotations to assist the reader in understanding the work.

revision.text:
{{Nihongo|'''Kasuga Gongen Genki E'''|春日権現験記絵}} is a set of painted handscrolls ({{ill|Emakimono|en|Emakimono|qid=Q1190781|lt=Emakimono|1=Emakimono}}) that was produced during the early 14th Century ({{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}) of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, by members of the Fujiwara clan. The work was created in order to honor the deities of Kasuga, with the stories revolving around the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga shrine]] and [[Kōfuku-ji|Kōfukuji temple]], located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The colourful silk work was made with silver and gold paints and consists of 20 scrolls with 93 sections of text and illustrations, which was completed in 1309.
[[File:Kasuga gongen genki-e, Takashina Takakane, 1-3 (part 1).JPG|thumb|Kasuga Gongen Genki E]]
==Meaning of the name== The term “Gongen” means {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|qid=Q83090|lt=Avatar|1=Avatar}} or [[Kami|Deity]], while the term “genki” in the title is actually short for “reigenki”; reigenki can be translated into “chronicles of marvelous and supernatural events”. Taken together, the title has been translated into “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity” or “Illustrated Tales of the Miracles of the Avatars of Kasuga” or the equivalent. And as the title suggests, the work focuses on miraculous events involving the Kasuga Shrine and Buddhist/Shinto deities.
==Authorship and history== The former regent ([[Sesshō and Kampaku|kanpaku]]), {{ill|Takatsukasa Mototada|en|Takatsukasa Mototada|qid=Q955290|lt=Takatsukasa (Fujiwara) Mototada|1=Takatsukasa Mototada}}, and his three sons wrote the original text, based on stories compiled by a monk named Kakuen of [[Tōbokuin]], in consultation with two other senior monks of [[Kōfuku-ji]] (Jishin of Daijōin and Hanken of Sanzōin).
Mototada copied out scrolls 1-5, 9-13 and 16; the eldest son, {{ill|Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|en|Takatsukasa Fuyuhira|qid=Q3514218|lt=Fuyuhira|1=Takatsukasa Fuyuhira}}, copied out scrolls 6-8; the second son, Fuyumoto, copied out scrolls 14, 15, 18 and 19; and the fourth son, Ryōshin, copied out scrolls 17 and 18. Takashina Takekane, who was the director of the imperial painting bureau (edokoro), created the illustrations. Following the completion of the work, the Minister of the Left ([[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]), {{ill|Saionji family|en|Saionji family|qid=Q7402472|lt=Saionji|1=Saionji family}} Kinhira (who was also a brother to Kakuen), dedicated it to the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] and neighboring {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhism}}, [[:en:Kōfukuji|Kōfukuji]], in order to honor the deities and thank them for honoring his home.
When the scrolls were first completed and kept at the Kasuga shrine, the scrolls were carefully protected—with no shrine priest or Kōfukuji monk less than 40 years of age able to view the scrolls. Moreover, if the scrolls were required at Kōfukuji, it was only the Tōbokuin sanctuary that was permitted to receive the scrolls. However, for a while, during the late 18th Century, the scrolls became less carefully looked after. The scrolls were most likely treated more casually because of the decline suffered by Kasuga and Kōfukuji at the time.
In order to make Kasuga Gongen genki e more accessible to the public, copies of the work began to appear during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. However, permission from the head of the [[Fujiwara clan]] was needed before any copies were allowed. Currently there are 6 known copies to be made: one owned by the Kajūji family, the {{ill|Yōmei Bunko|en|Yōmei Bunko|qid=Q11658779|lt=Yōmei Bunko|1=Yōmei Bunko}} copy (Yōmei Bunko-bon), the Kasuga copy (Kasuga-bon/Kuwana-bon), the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}} copy (Tōkyō Kokuritsu Hakubutsukan-bon) no. 1, the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|qid=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library|1=National Diet Library}} copy (Kokuritsu Kokkai Toshokan-bon), and the Tokyo National Museum copy no. 2 (3). The original work is of extremely high quality and is kept by the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial household|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.
==English Translation== {{ill|Royall Tyler (academic)|en|Royall Tyler (academic)|qid=Q3826510|lt=Royall Tyler|1=Royall Tyler (academic)}} has completed a study and complete English translation of Kasuga Gongen genki e, under the title “The Miracles of the Kasuga Deity”, which was published in 1990. Within his translation, Royall Tyler refers to Kasuga Gongen genki as “the Genki”. The first part of the book gives background information on the Kasuga shrine, Kōfukuji, the Kasuga cult and the religious background and context in which the original work was created. The second part of the book gives a complete translation of all 20 scrolls, with annotations to assist the reader in understanding the work.
==See also== *{{ill|Japanese painting|en|Japanese painting|qid=Q1683295|lt=Japanese painting|1=Japanese painting}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|qid=Q3245518|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}}
